skill.name: waapi
skill.description: Web Animations API adapter patterns for HyperFrames. Use when authoring element.animate() motion, Animation currentTime seeking, document.getAnimations(), KeyframeEffect timing, fill modes, or native browser animations that must render deterministically in HyperFrames.

security_audit.summary: All 26 static analysis findings are false positives. The SKILL.md file contains only documentation with standard Markdown formatting. The 18 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' flags are Markdown code fences (```html, ```js, ```bash) and inline code spans (`text`) — no shell execution exists. The 2 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are the YAML frontmatter --- separator. The 3 'System reconnaissance' flags are innocuous documentation text about animation best practices. The 2 'Hardcoded URL' flags are legitimate MDN documentation references. This is a documentation-only skill with no executable code, network requests, or dangerous patterns.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 19 instances of markdown code fences (```html, ```js, ```bash) and inline code spans (`text`) as Ruby shell backtick execution. These are standard Markdown formatting constructs in a documentation-only file. No actual shell execution occurs. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: YAML frontmatter flagged as weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged the YAML frontmatter --- separator (line 3) as a weak cryptographic algorithm indicator. This is the standard Markdown YAML frontmatter delimiter. No cryptographic content exists. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Documentation text flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged lines containing words like 'Avoid' and 'Lightweight' as system reconnaissance. These are normal English words in documentation describing animation best practices and use cases. No reconnaissance intent or capability exists. FALSE POSITIVE.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs flagged as network threat
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged two MDN documentation URLs as hardcoded network endpoints. These are reference links to Mozilla Developer Network documentation for the Web Animations API and Animation.currentTime. Legitimate documentation references. FALSE POSITIVE.

content.user_title: Animate Web Elements for HyperFrames Video
content.value_statement: HyperFrames compositions need deterministic animation seeking for frame-accurate video rendering. This skill provides WAAPI adapter patterns that work with the HyperFrames time-seeking render pipeline without external animation libraries.
actual_capabilities: Creates WAAPI animations compatible with HyperFrames frame seek
Uses element.animate() with finite duration, fill modes, and cubic-bezier easing
Supports staggered animations using querySelectorAll with incremental delays
Pauses animations after creation for deterministic time seeking
Provides keyframe animations with multi-step offset sequences
Guides users on timing configuration for clip-level synchronization
limitations: Cannot use infinite iteration animations (not compatible with seek)
Does not support promise-based callbacks for render-critical state
Cannot use requestAnimationFrame or separate clocks alongside WAAPI
Requires manual delay configuration for clip offset timing

use_cases.title: Add motion to video compositions
use_cases.description: Video creators using HyperFrames need deterministic DOM animations that render correctly at each seeked frame. This skill shows how to build WAAPI animations that the HyperFrames render pipeline can control.
use_cases.target_user: Video content creators and motion designers

use_cases.title: Replace GSAP with lightweight WAAPI
use_cases.description: Web developers who want native browser animations without GSAP dependency can use this skill to create performant keyframe animations using the Web Animations API directly.

use_cases.title: Generate animations from structured data
use_cases.description: Engineers building automated video pipelines need programmatic animation generation. This skill provides the WAAPI patterns needed to create animations from JSON or JavaScript data structures.
use_cases.target_user: Software engineers building automated HyperFrames pipelines

prompt_templates.title: Basic WAAPI animation setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple WAAPI animation for a div element that fades in and moves from left to center. Use the standard HyperFrames WAAPI pattern with finite duration, fill: both, and pause after creation.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user creating a straightforward animation for a HyperFrames video composition

prompt_templates.title: Staggered list animation
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a list of 10 items that I want to animate in sequentially with a WAAPI stagger. Each item should fade in and slide up. Use querySelectorAll and forEach with incremental delays.
prompt_templates.scenario: User animating multiple elements with a sequential delay pattern

prompt_templates.title: Multi-step keyframe animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a WAAPI animation for a logo element that scales, rotates, and changes opacity at different keyframe offsets. Use at least 4 keyframe stops with cubic-bezier easing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Motion designer creating a complex multi-step animation sequence

prompt_templates.title: Debugging animation seek timing
prompt_templates.prompt: My WAAPI animations are not seeking correctly in the HyperFrames preview. The elements appear at wrong times or do not show at all. Help me debug the timing configuration and verify the adapter is applying currentTime correctly.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user troubleshooting animation timing issues in the HyperFrames render pipeline

output_examples.input: Create a fade-in title animation using WAAPI
output_examples.output: The skill generates a WAAPI animation pattern where your title element uses element.animate() with opacity and transform keyframes at specific offsets. The animation is created synchronously, uses fill: both so the title remains visible when seeked, and is paused after creation so HyperFrames controls playback position through the waapi runtime adapter.

output_examples.input: How do I animate a group of cards with staggered timing and no GSAP dependency?
output_examples.output: The skill walks through a stagger pattern using querySelectorAll on your card elements. Each card gets its own WAAPI animation with the same keyframes but incremental delay values. All animations use finite iterations, fill: both, and are paused after creation to work with HyperFrames deterministic seeking.

output_examples.input: My animation is not visible when I seek to the correct time position
output_examples.output: The skill identifies that the animation may be missing the fill: both property, which is required for seeked states to persist. It also checks that delay matches the clip data-start attribute, that duration is finite, and that the animation is paused immediately after creation.

best_practices: Create all animations synchronously during composition initialization before the first seek operation
Use finite duration and iterations values so the animation has a known end state for seeking
Set fill: both on all animations so seeked states persist at the correct visual position
anti_patterns: Using infinite iterations makes animations unseekable and causes unpredictable visual states
Depending on promise-based callbacks like animation.finished for render-critical DOM mutations introduces timing bugs
Running separate clocks with requestAnimationFrame or performance.now() conflicts with the WAAPI adapter seek control

faq.question: What is WAAPI and why use it with HyperFrames?
faq.answer: WAAPI stands for Web Animations API, a native browser API for creating animations with JavaScript. HyperFrames uses a WAAPI runtime adapter that seeks animations to specific times for frame-accurate video rendering.

faq.question: How does the WAAPI adapter seek animations?
faq.answer: The adapter calls document.getAnimations() to find all active animations, sets each animation's currentTime to the HyperFrames time in milliseconds, and then pauses it. This produces the correct visual state for each frame.

faq.question: Can I use infinite iterations with WAAPI in HyperFrames?
faq.answer: No. Infinite iterations are not compatible with the seek-based render model. Always set a finite iterations value so the animation has a predictable end state.

faq.question: How do I handle staggered animations for multiple elements?
faq.answer: Use querySelectorAll to select all target elements and create individual WAAPI animations with incremental delay values. Each animation uses the same keyframes but different delay based on the element index.

faq.question: What is the difference between WAAPI and GSAP in HyperFrames?
faq.answer: WAAPI uses native browser keyframes and requires no external library dependency. GSAP provides more advanced timeline control and easing options. Both have HyperFrames runtime adapters for deterministic seeking.

faq.question: How do I debug WAAPI animations that are not showing at the correct time?
faq.answer: Check that your animation delay matches the clip data-start attribute, that fill is set to both, that duration is finite, and that the animation is paused after creation. Run npx hyperframes lint and npx hyperframes validate to catch common issues.
